nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving to moderately flowing tributaries and streams of the Río Acandí drainage on the Caribbean slope; shaded water with leaf litter and submerged vegetation

decorPreferences: dense background planting (Amazon sword, Vallisneria, hornwort)
open swimming space in the midwater
driftwood and leaf litter for tannins and cover
dark substrate to bring out metallic colouration

diet.notes: Unfussy and readily accepts most aquarium foods. Offer a varied diet of high-quality flake or micro-pellets as a staple, supplemented with frozen or live foods such as bloodworm, daphnia, brine shrimp, and mosquito larvae. Vegetable-based flake can be offered occasionally. Feed small amounts 1–2 times daily.

goodWith: medium-sized tetras (e.g., Buenos Aires Tetra, Black Skirt Tetra)
cichlids of similar size and temperament (e.g., keyhole cichlid, German blue ram in larger tanks)
avoidWith: long-finned varieties of any species (excessive fin-nipping)
small or delicate tetras that may be bullied
dwarf shrimp (likely to be predated or harassed)
very small fish that may be eaten

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo of Hyphessobrycon columbianus showing characteristic blue body and red fins.

summary: A large, bold tetra from Colombia's Caribbean drainages with a striking metallic-blue body and vivid red fins. Described only in 2002, it is robust and easy to feed but is a confirmed fin-nipper — keep in groups of 8 or more and avoid long-finned tankmates.
careNotes: The Colombian Tetra is one of the larger tetras available to the hobby, with adults typically reaching 6–7 cm. Its metallic blue flanks and bright red fins make it one of the most visually striking characins. Described by Zarske and Géry in 2002, it remained relatively obscure in the trade for some years before gaining wider availability. Despite its attractive appearance, the Colombian Tetra has a well-earned reputation as a fin-nipper. Groups of fewer than 6–8 individuals tend to be significantly more aggressive, as intra-species competition focuses aggression within the shoal rather than outward; a minimum group of 8 is strongly recommended to diffuse this behaviour. Long-finned tankmates (bettas, fancy guppies, Angelfish, long-finned Danios) should be avoided entirely. A tank of at least 100 L (90 cm footprint) is needed to accommodate a proper shoal with adequate swimming room; a 120 L or larger tank is preferable. Maintain soft-to-moderately-hard, slightly acidic to neutral water. The species adapts reasonably well to neutral, moderately hard water and is not as demanding in this regard as blackwater specialists. A well-filtered, well-oxygenated tank with moderate flow is appreciated. Subdued lighting with floating plants and a dark substrate will enhance the fish's iridescent colouration. The Colombian Tetra is an active, confident swimmer and benefits from open mid-water swimming space alongside areas of dense planting.
breedingNotes: Breeding in the aquarium is possible but not routinely achieved by hobbyists. Like most tetras, Colombian Tetras are egg scatterers that show no parental care and will readily consume their own eggs. Conditioning a small group (2–3 pairs) on a high-protein live and frozen diet for several weeks before moving them to a dedicated breeding tank (soft, slightly acidic water, pH 6.0–6.5, temperature 25–26°C, very dim lighting, fine-leaved plants or a spawning mop, bare bottom) is the standard approach. Remove adults after spawning. Eggs hatch within 24–36 hours; fry become free-swimming around day 4–5 and can initially be fed infusoria or commercial fry food, graduating to micro-worms and baby brine shrimp.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Hyphessobrycon columbianus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (~6 cm), temperature range 22–27°C, pH 5.5–7.5, hardness 18–268 ppm (approx. gH 1–15), native range (Río Acandí drainage, Caribbean Colombia), habitat description, diet, behaviour notes (fin-nipper, group of 6+), and breeding outline.

additional.name: FishBase — Hyphessobrycon columbianus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Characidae, Characiformes), adult size (max ~7 cm TL), native range (Colombia, Caribbean slope), original description citation (Zarske & Géry 2002).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Colombian Tetra Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade care guide confirming size (up to 3 inches / ~7.5 cm), temperature 72–82°F (22–28°C), pH 6.0–7.5, minimum tank 20 gal (75 L) for small groups but 30 gal+ recommended, omnivore diet, fin-nipper reputation, and group size recommendation of 6–8+.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Hyphessobrycon columbianus species profile
additional.notes: Additional UK trade source confirming care parameters, semi-aggressive temperament, fin-nipping behaviour, and recommendation to keep in large groups to manage aggression.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterer; breeds in soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0–6.5, 25–26°C) with fine-leaved plants or spawning mop. No parental care — adults must be removed after spawning. Fry free-swimming ~day 4–5; feed infusoria then baby brine shrimp.
